author: Augustine of Hippo
book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
On the Morals of the Manichæans.
What Evil is. That Evil is that Which is Against Nature. In Allowing This, the Manichæans Refute Themselves.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

title: What Evil is. That Evil is that Which is Against Nature. In Allowing This, the Manichæans Refute Themselves.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: You Manichæans often, if not in every case, ask those whom you try to bring over to your heresy, Whence is evil?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Suppose I had now met you for the first time, I would ask you, if you please, to follow my example in putting aside for a little the explanation you suppose yourselves to have got of these subjects, and to commence this great inquiry with me as if for the first time.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: You ask me, Whence is evil?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: I ask you in return, What is evil?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Which is the more reasonable question?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Are those right who ask whence a thing is, when they do not know what it is; or he who thinks it necessary to inquire first what it is, in order to avoid the gross absurdity of searching for the origin of a thing unknown?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Your answer is quite correct, when you say that evil is that which is contrary to nature; for no one is so mentally blind as not to see that, in every kind, evil is that which is contrary to the nature of the kind.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: But the establishment of this doctrine is the overthrow of your heresy.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: For evil is no nature, if it is contrary to nature.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Now, according to you, evil is a certain nature and substance.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Moreover, whatever is contrary to nature must oppose nature and seek its destruction.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: For nature means nothing else than that which anything is conceived of as being in its own kind.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Hence is the new word which we now use derived from the word for being,—essence namely, or, as we usually say, substance,—while before these words were in use, the word nature was used instead.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Here, then, if you will consider the matter without stubbornness, we see that evil is that which falls away from essence and tends to non-existence.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Accordingly, when the Catholic Church declares that God is the author of all natures and substances, those who understand this understand at the same time that God is not the author of evil.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: For how can He who is the cause of the being of all things be at the same time the cause of their not being,—that is, of their falling off from essence and tending to non-existence?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: For this is what reason plainly declares to be the definition of evil.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Now, how can that race of evil of yours, which you make the supreme evil, be against nature, that is, against substance, when it, according to you, is itself a nature and substance?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: For if it acts against itself, it destroys its own existence; and when that is completely done, it will come at last to be the supreme evil.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: But this cannot be done, because you will have it not only to be, but to be everlasting.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: That cannot then be the chief evil which is spoken of as a substance.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: But what am I to do?

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: I know that many of you can understand nothing of all this.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: I know, too, that there are some who have a good understanding and can see these things, and yet are so stubborn in their choice of evil,—a choice that will ruin their understanding as well,—that they try rather to find what reply they can make in order to impose upon inactive and feeble minds, instead of giving their assent to the truth.

verses.book: On the Morals of the Manichæans.
verses.text: Still I shall not regret having written either what one of you may come some day to consider impartially, and be led to abandon your error, or what men of understanding and in allegiance to God, and who are still untainted with your errors, may read and so be kept from being led astray by your addresses.
